coredata.dc:title: Physico-chemical properties and biological evaluation of graphene quantum dots for anticancer drug susceptibility

coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) possess unique optical and biocompatible properties, making them suitable candidates for biomedical and pharmaceutical applications. This study reports the hydrothermal synthesis of pristine-GQD and doped variants: Nitrogen-GQD and Sulfur-GQD. The materials underwent thorough characterization techniques such as UV–vis, fluorescence, XRD, FE-TEM/SEM, EDX, and Raman spectroscopy. The particle sizes of these GQDs range from 2 to 5 nm. We conducted a comprehensive study through MTT assays to evaluate the potential cytotoxic effect of GQD and the doped variants. This study demonstrated their synergistic interactions with an anti-cancer drug, methotrexate (MTX), and also improvement of cytocompatibility in the presence of folic acid (FA). Systematic MD simulations revealed a compacting effect on the dynamic behavior of GQD and its variants in the presence of drugs. Fluorescence spectroscopy and computational modeling suggest non-intercalative surface interactions between GQDs and the drugs. The cytotoxic activity of pristine GQD on HeLa cervical cancer cells is higher than that of N-GQD and S-GQD. When treated with GQD-IC50–MTX-IC50, only 5.6 % of HeLa cells remained viable. The doped variants exhibited bio-compatibility when tested on normal HEK cell lines. Overall, this study emphasizes the potential of GQDs for targeted cancer therapy through an interdisciplinary approach involving material characterization, computational modeling, and biological assays.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FE-TEM image of (A) GQD, (B) N-GQD, and (C) S-GQD. The inset in each image shows a more detailed view of the quantum dot’s structure at a higher magnification. (D-F) Quantitative descriptions of the particle sizes are shown as the histogram and distribution for each system.

The UV–vis spectra of (A) GQD, (B) N-GQD, and (C) S-GQD are displayed with an arrow indicating the peak position corresponding to the π-π* transition type. The Tauc-Plot of (D) GQD, (E) N-GQD, and (F) S-GQD represents the band-gap energy of molecules. The Raman spectrum shows the position of D and G bands in (G) GQD, (H) N-GQD, and (I) S-GQD.

Fluorescence spectra of (A) GQD, (B) N-GQD, and (C) S-GQD at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 350 nm to 430 nm. The overall shifts in the FL peak position (extreme blue to red-shifted emission peaks) are shown with dotted lines. Time-resolved PL spectra showing the decay plot of (D) GQD, (E) N-GQD, and (F) S-GQD at excitation and emission wavelengths of 375 and 525 nm, respectively. The average lifetime (Avg. τ) for each fluorescence decay is shown as inset.

UV–vis spectra of Methotrexate (MTX) with (A) GQD, (B) N-GQD, and (C) S-GQD. Fluorescence spectra (excitation wavelength 350 nm) of MTX with (D) GQD, (B) N-GQD, and (C) S-GQD. UV–vis spectra of Folic acid (FA) with (G) GQD, (H) N-GQD, and (I) S-GQD. Fluorescence spectra (excitation wavelength 350 nm) of FA mixed with (J) GQD, (K) N-GQD, and (L) S-GQD. The arrow marks in fluorescence spectra show the trend in intensity with incremental concentration.

RMSD distribution of GQD and N/S-dopant (A) without any drug; (B) in the presence of FA; (C) in the presence of MTX. The density profile for molecular packing with reference to the center of GQDs (D) without any drug; (E) in the presence of FA; (F) in the presence of MTX. The dot plot shows a correlation between the number of hydrogen bonds and contact area formed with GQDs and the N/S-doped system with (G) FA and (H) MTX. Some representative snapshots from the trajectory of molecular dynamics are shown with arrows.

Cell viability of HeLa cells with treatment of (A) GQD, (B) N-GQD, (C) S-GQD; and of HEK cells with treatment of (D) GQD, (E) N-GQD, (F) S-GQD. Cell viability with combination treatment of GQD-MTX of (G) HeLa and (J) HEK cells; N-GQD-MTX of (H) HeLa and (K) HEK cells; S-GQD-MTX of (I) HeLa and (L) HEK cells. All results were obtained after 48 hours of incubation using MTT dye. The results are expressed as the mean ± SEM for three independent experiments. The statistical significance was compared to the untreated cells. The significance level was set at p < 0.05 (*), p < 0.01 (**), p < 0.001 (***), p < 0.0001 (****).
Comparative cell viability percentage of HeLa and HEK cells after treatment with (a) GQD-MTX, (b) N-GQD-MTX, and (c) S-GQD-MTX for 48 hours.

head.ce:title: Physico-chemical properties and biological evaluation of graphene quantum dots for anticancer drug susceptibility

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) possess unique optical and biocompatible properties, making them suitable candidates for biomedical and pharmaceutical applications. This study reports the hydrothermal synthesis of pristine-GQD and doped variants: Nitrogen-GQD and Sulfur-GQD. The materials underwent thorough characterization techniques such as UV–vis, fluorescence, XRD, FE-TEM/SEM, EDX, and Raman spectroscopy. The particle sizes of these GQDs range from 2 to 5 nm. We conducted a comprehensive study through MTT assays to evaluate the potential cytotoxic effect of GQD and the doped variants. This study demonstrated their synergistic interactions with an anti-cancer drug, methotrexate (MTX), and also improvement of cytocompatibility in the presence of folic acid (FA). Systematic MD simulations revealed a compacting effect on the dynamic behavior of GQD and its variants in the presence of drugs. Fluorescence spectroscopy and computational modeling suggest non-intercalative surface interactions between GQDs and the drugs. The cytotoxic activity of pristine GQD on HeLa cervical cancer cells is higher than that of N-GQD and S-GQD. When treated with GQD-IC50–MTX-IC50, only 5.6 % of HeLa cells remained viable. The doped variants exhibited bio-compatibility when tested on normal HEK cell lines. Overall, this study emphasizes the potential of GQDs for targeted cancer therapy through an interdisciplinary approach involving material characterization, computational modeling, and biological assays.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of graphene quantum dots and their cytotoxic evaluation through MTT assay.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Raw files of all the measurement and simulation data are available in the Zenodo digital repository (DOI: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.13935472).

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QD) have emerged as potential candidates for bio-imaging, medical diagnosis, and pharmaceutical applications [1–3]. They typically consist of crystalline semiconducting materials with dimensions ranging from a few nanometers [4]. QDs are renowned for their exceptional optical and electronic properties; their tightly confined electrons result in quantum mechanical effects that distinguish them from larger particles. The ability to synthesize QDs marked a significant breakthrough, contributing to the successful advancement of nanoscience and nanotechnology. The versatile role of QDs in everyday life, including electronics and therapeutics, led to the awarding of the Nobel Prize in Chemistry 2023 to Moungi G. Bawendi, Louis E. Brus, and Alexei I. Ekimov [5].

When QDs are exposed to UV radiation, an electron moves to an excited state, transitioning from the valance band to the conduction band, leading to the release of energy when it returns to the lower energy states, known as photoluminescence. Importantly, the tunable band gap impacts the optoelectronic properties of QDs based on the size and shape [4]. Larger QDs (approximately 5–6 nm in diameter) emit longer wavelengths, producing colors like orange or red, while smaller QDs (2–3 nm in diameter) emit shorter wavelengths, resulting in colors such as blue and green [6]. Furthermore, the specific color emitted is influenced by the exact composition of the QDs [7].

Since its emergence in 2008, Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have demonstrated remarkable versatility with superior solubility, as well as unique optical and electrical conductivity properties [8]. Unlike traditional carbonaceous materials such as graphite, carbon nanotubes and fullerene, GQDs have pronounced quantum confinement and edge effect [9]. Additionally, GQDs are zero-dimensional materials with ultrafine structures, zero band gap, larger surface area, dispersibility, stable photoluminescence, and a size comparable to biomolecules, which distinguishes them as a valuable material in comparison to other alternatives. A significant advantage of GQDs is their reduced toxicity and biocompatibility, making them ideal for bioimaging and drug delivery applications [10–13]. The honeycomb-like lattice structure (sp2 hybridization) is responsible for their high crystallinity [14]. Additionally, their properties can be tuned through physical factors (e.g., pH, temperature, pressure) to introduce structural defects and through chemical methods (e.g., acid or base treatment) for functionalization [15]. Advanced synthesis methods also allow for doping with single, double, or even multiple hetero atoms to enhance their application.

In this study, GQDs were synthesized using a top-down approach from graphene oxide (GO) via the hydrothermal method [16–20]. Notable achievements in this area include the successful synthesis of surface passivated GQDs with a quantum yield of 7.4 % by Shen et al., demonstrating characteristic blue fluorescence [21]. Pan et al. also reported a hydrothermal route for GQDs preparation, achieving a QY of 7 % and 10 nm particle sizes [22]. Additionally, Cai et al. used ultrasonication to synthesize N-GQDs from graphite precursors with hydrazine for N doping, resulting in an average diameter of ca. 2.5 nm [23]. Likewise, Ju et al. fabricated blue luminescent N-GQDs with an impressive QY of 32.4 %, showcasing the potential applications in detecting intracellular glutathione levels in cancer cells [24]. Bian et al. used a similar procedure for S-GQDs synthesis, employing 1,3,6-trinitropyrene for C and 3-mercaptopropionic acid for S doping [25]. Sangam et al. synthesized S-GQDs from direct waste biomass via the same route, achieving a high QY of ∼47 %, and the materials were viable as bioimaging probes [26]. GQDs high surface area enables interactions including hydrophobic, π–π stacking, and electrostatics with wide range of organic molecules including drugs, making them attractive candidates for biomedical applications [27].

Additionally, we also synthesized two GQD derivatives, N-GQD and S-GQD, and examined their optical and structural properties. The N and S doping are studied to investigate the effect enhanced electron density over the quantum mechanical properties of quantum dots. Our primary objective was to assess the potential of these zero-dimensional materials when employed in the anticancer drug targeting cervical cancer. We specifically focused on evaluating the efficacy of methotrexate (MTX), a well-established treatment for cervical cancer, and its substrate analogue - folic acid (FA), known for its efficacy against breast cancer. The hydrothermal synthesis of GQD using GO and cysteine precursors is an unique aspect of this work. Further, we examined the material’s bio-compatibility when tested on normal HEK cell lines and anti-cancerous activity on HeLa cell lines. We utilized optical spectroscopy and molecular dynamics simulation to gain insights into the stability of GQDs in the presence of these drugs. The experimental validation of GQD with MTX and FA by observing their IC50 values against the HeLa cell line derived from cervical cancer cells substantiates the objective of this work. Furthermore, the effectiveness of MTX against these cancer cell lines using the MTT assay underscores the potential activity of GODs for cervical cancer treatment.

Graphite powder (Alfa Aesar), 30 % w/v hydrogen peroxide, potassium permanganate, sodium nitrate, concentrated sulphuric acid (18.77 M), and ammonia solution (14.7 M) were purchased from FINAR. L-Cysteine (≥98.5 %) from Merck was also used for the material synthesis. All the chemicals were used without further purification and are of analytical grade unless otherwise stated.

GO was synthesized using the Hummer method [28]. In a 500 mL beaker, 1 g of graphite powder, 0.5 g of sodium nitrate, and 23 mL of sulphuric acid were mixed and vigorously stirred at 25 °C for 15 min [29]. The beaker was then placed in a water bath to maintain a constant temperature. Subsequently, 3 g of potassium permanganate was slowly added to the solution, followed by cooling for 15 min. The suspension was continuously stirred for 1 h, and then 46 mL of deionized (DI) water was slowly added for 10 min. Afterward, the suspension was diluted with 140 mL of warm water and treated with 10 mL of H2O2 to reduce the residual permanganate to soluble manganese ions. Finally, the resulting suspension was filtered, washed with DI water, and dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 24 hours to obtain GO.

To synthesize GQD, 15 mg of pre-synthesized GO precursors were mixed with 11 mL, 5 % w/v H2O2, and transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave reactor. The reactor was then heated in a hot air oven at 180 °C for 2 hr. After cooling to ambient temperature, the resulting solution was filtered using a Whatman filter paper (grade-1) to remove the unreacted GO, and the GQD filtrate was collected. The sample was stored in a dark condition, wrapped with aluminum foil at 4 °C. To synthesize N-GQD, 2.5 mg of pre-synthesized GO was dissolved in 20 mL of DI water, and 2 mL of NH4OH (nitrogen source) was added. The mixture was then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was filtered using the same process, and the sample was stored as previously described. For the synthesis of S-GQD, the same GO (31 mg) was used. The method was similar to that used for N-GQD, except L-Cysteine (29 mg) was used as the S-source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characterization experiments – UV–vis and Fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: We measured absorbance using a Labman UV–vis spectrophotometer (Model No. LMSP-UV1900S). Additionally, we recorded the UV–visible spectra of two specific drugs (methotrexate and folate) at incremental concentrations in the GQD systems. Our fluorescence measurements were carried out using the Horiba Fluoromax-4 Spectrofluorometer. Furthermore, we performed time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy using a ps-time-resolved and steady-state luminescence spectrometer, Edinburg Instrument, Lifespec II & FSP 920. A 1 cm path, four-sided transparent cuvette with uniform thickness to measure the fluorescence spectra. Our fluorescence lifetime measurements allowed us to study the change in fluorescence of samples over time when irradiated with UV–vis or near IR radiation. Incremental drug solutions were added to record the emission properties. Both drugs were used at a solution concentration of 1 mg/mL, and the solution was stored appropriately to prevent degradation from light and temperature [30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization experiments – XRD, FE-TEM, FE-SEM, EDX, and Raman
ce:section.ce:para: We utilized Bruker D8 ADVANCE ECO P-XRD system with a Cu-Kα X-ray irradiation source to record the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns. Morphological characterization of GQDs was conducted using Field Emission Transmission Electron Microscopy (FE-TEM) with a JEOL 2100 F instrument to ascertain their shape and size. For imaging, a 5 μl drop of GQD dispersion solution was deposited on the dark carbon-facing side of the copper grid.

Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM) images were captured using a Sigma-300 electron microscope (Zeiss Sigma) with magnifications of 10X to 300,000X. In preparation, a 1×1 cm2 silicon wafer was cleaned with warm acetone for 10–15 min at 50°C, then placed in methanol for 3 min, rinsed with DI water, and treated with freshly prepared Piranha solution. The wafer was thoroughly washed with DI water and dried on a hot plate. The GQDs dispersion solution was drop-cast onto the cleaned thin film silicon wafer surface and left to dry overnight on a hot plate at 30 °C. Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) morphological analysis was performed using the same instrument. Raman spectroscopy was conducted using a LabRAM HR Evolution, a HORIBA Scientific instrument, with an acquisition time of 10 seconds and a frequency range of 399–4000 cm−1. A 532 nm excitation wavelength laser was used for the characterization.

We conducted a cell viability assay to evaluate the cytotoxicity of GQDs using the calorimetric MTT method. Initially, 10,000 cells per well were seeded and allowed to incubate for 24 hrs before treatment. Both the HeLa cervical cancer cell line and HEK normal cell line were used for the assay and were exposed to different concentrations of GQDs. After a 48-hrs treatment period, we performed the MTT assay, which involves the reduction of MTT dye by the respiratory mitochondrial enzyme in living cells, resulting in the formation of blue formazan products. The absorbance was measured at a wavelength of 570 nm using the GloMax plate reader, with 600 nm as the reference filter wavelength.

A similar cell viability assay was conducted to assess the combined effect of the MTX and GQDs on both cell lines. Three different combinations of MTX and GQDs, i.e., GQDs IC50–MTX IC50, GQDs IC25–MTX IC75, and GQDs IC75–MTX IC25. Further, 500 µg/mL of folic acid was used in combination with GQD-MTX for the treatment to determine the cytotoxic impact of this nano-formulation, and the MTT assay was performed similarly as described earlier. Data analysis was carried out using GraphPad Prism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modeling of GQDs and classical simulations
ce:section.ce:para: The quantum dot was initially created using 7 sheets of carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal pattern, with a hydroxyl functional group, using the “Carbon Dot Builder” program. The theoretical models of quantum dots are in accordance to the protocol reported by Paloncýová et. al. [31], in which the sheet increases in size towards the center, forming a spherical shape. The multi-layered structure enhances the surface area and mimics the real-world condition of graphene quantum dots for drug adsorption. The structure was then modified using the Maestro suite [32] to include carboxyl groups at the edges and epoxy groups within the sheet. N- and S-doped systems were also developed by replacing the C atom and adjusting the atomic valency [33]. The chemical structure of anticancerous drugs used in this study was obtained from PubChem and combined with GQDs for the simulation setup. The interactions of GQDs with MTX and FA were analyzed using molecular dynamics simulations with the OPLS_2005 force field [34]. The force-field has optimized parameters for conjugated system, giving reliable description hydrogen bonding, π-π stacking, solvation impact, and vdW forces, which are experimentally validated [35,36]. Thus, OPLS provides comprehensive interaction insights, which are crucial for understanding the graphene quantum dot mechanical and chemical properties. Nine systems (Table S1) were then simulated using Desmond [37]. For more detailed information on the classical all-atom simulations, refer to the supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The particle size of S-GQDs is significantly smaller
ce:section.ce:para: We conducted a detailed morphological characterization of GQDs using FE-TEM to determine their shape and size. Fig. 1 demonstrates that all systems exhibit homogeneous and spherical structures, indicating monodispersed properties. The average particle size of GQDs is 3.07 nm, and the introduction of N into the graphene lattice slightly increases the overall size to 4.82 nm. In contrast, S-GQD shows a relatively smaller particle size at 2.22 nm, with significant impacts on surface properties. The change in particle size could be attributed to the changes in surface functionalization, that is reduction in oxygen-containing functional groups in N-GQD and S-GQD. High-resolution images at a 5 nm scale are included in the inset of Fig. 1A-C, showing the lattice fringes of the QDs, which correspond to the characteristic interplanar d-spacing of the (002) plane, confirming the XRD finding. The interplanar spacing for GQDs is nearly 0.32 nm, decreased to around 0.26 nm in N-GQD, and increased to 0.39 nm in S-GQD. These variations in lattice fringe spacing are attributed to the atomic radii of nitrogen, which is slightly smaller, and sulfur, which is higher than that of carbon.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The distinct crystalline pattern of GQDs is attributed to N and S-doping
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD patterns offer valuable insights into the crystallinity and characteristic peaks of quantum dots. In Fig. S1(A), it is observed that the GQDs display two distinct peaks at 2Ɵ = 23.88˚ for the (002) plane and at 2Ɵ = 44.2˚ for the (004) plane, signifying the restoration of the carbon network system [38]. An additional peak at 2Ɵ = 11.82˚ corresponds to oxygen functionalities [39]. Moving to Fig. S1(B), the diffraction pattern of N-GQD reveals a peak at 2Ɵ = 20.58˚ for the (002) plane, slightly shifted compared to GQD, possibly due to structural deformation caused by nitrogen functionalities. The (004) plane is negligible in N-GQD. Additionally, Fig. S1(C) demonstrates the characteristic peak at 2Ɵ = 22.88˚ (002) for S-GQD, which is also shifted to lower Bragg’s angle than GQD. However, the other characteristic peaks for the (004) plane at 2Ɵ = 46˚ are shifted to a higher value. These results attributed to the structural deformation in S-GQD due to cysteine functionality. The additional peaks at 2Ɵ values of 17.06˚, 28.62˚, 29.8 ˚, 35.68˚, 38.82˚, and 41.72˚ are also observed due to the presence of crystalline cysteine on QD’s surface, which is in line to earlier reports [40].

Insights gained from FE-SEM images offer valuable comparisons of the morphological and topographical features of GQDs. The discernible spherical-like structure of GQDs and their variable levels of agglomeration can be attributed to the introduction of diverse functional groups during the doping process (Fig. S2, upper panel). The morphologies of N-doped GQDs and regular GODs exhibit similarities, suggesting the presence of strong van der Waals forces. Despite the alteration in surface chemistry due to N-doping, they demonstrate agglomeration tendencies akin to GQDs, aligning with XRD-based crystalline analysis. Interestingly, the FE-SEM images distinctly reveal S-GQD particles, validating the reduced particle size observed. Notably, S-doping induces stabilizing effects, ensuring particle separation and resulting in weaker particle-particle interactions.

The EDX technique, in conjunction with electron microscopy, has provided comprehensive insights into the elementary composition and spatial distribution of GQDs, as illustrated in Fig. S2, lower panel. It is noteworthy that the composition of O was nearly 25 % in both GQD and N-GQD, whereas in S-GQD, it was 9.8 %, indicating a variance in chemical stability due to S-doping and reduction treatment. Furthermore, the spectral image of N-GQD unveiled a faint signal between C and O, while the C content in S-GQD was relatively higher at 44.3 % compared to GQD (17.7 %) and N-GQD (14.9 %). The doping percentages for N and S in their respective systems are 2.5 % and 31.5 % (Table S2), a distinction that could be attributed to the reactivity and availability of the precursors (NH4OH for N-GQD and Cysteine for S-GQD).

ce:section.ce:section-title: S-GQD exhibits a higher band gap and amorphous characteristics
ce:section.ce:para: In GQD, two predominant electronic transition bands, assigned as π-π* and n-π* transitions [41], are typically observed. The π-π* transition originates from the aromatic CC network, whereas the n-π* transition is associated with the lone pair electrons in oxygen-containing moieties. In the context of GQDs, our analysis revealed a pronounced peak at 345 nm accompanied by a lower intensity shoulder peak at 370 nm, aligning with π-π* and n-π* transitions, respectively ( Fig. 2A). This observation is coherent with spectra previously reported for GQDs [42]. It is noteworthy that doping with N- and S-doped introduces an additional set of lone pair electrons, thereby augmenting the π-electron density. Nonetheless, the absorbance pattern of N-GQD did not exhibit substantial deviation from that of pristine GQDs (Fig. 2B). A minor red-shift of 5 nm (350 nm) for the π-π* transition was noted in N-GQD. In contrast, S-GQD demonstrated a significant blue shift, approximately 120 nm, culminating in an absorbance peak at 225 nm (Fig. 2C). The disparity between N and S-doped systems can be ascribed to variations in the quantum dots size [43,44]. These spectra suggest that S-GQD comprises comparatively smaller particles, thereby elevating the energy gap. The energy differential of S-GQD relative to GQD and N-GQD is quantified as 1.92 and 1.97 eV, respectively. The low-energy peak, indicative of the n-π* transition in N-GQD, encompasses a broader spectral range between 440 – 470 nm, whereas, in S-GQD, it is localized around 280 nm. This discrepancy underscores the propensity of N atoms lone pairs to conjugate with graphene CC skeleton, as opposed to S atoms. Orbital mixing, depicted as [(2p)N - (2p)C], is deemed more favorable over [(3p)S - (2p)C], considering the factors of orbital symmetry, size, and energetic considerations. The band gap, defining the energy span between the ground and excited states for GQDs, was evaluated using the Tauc Plot (Fig. 2D-F). Remarkably, the band gap of S-GQD (5.17 eV) significantly surpasses those of N-GQD (2.94 eV) and GQD (3.55 eV), a correlation that is consistent with the smaller particle size as observed through FE-TEM analysis. The notable blue shift in S-GQD compared to other GQDs could be attributed to two main reasons. First is the quantum confinement effect, as sulfur atoms brings distinct electronic states compared to nitrogen atoms. This effects the band gap of graphene quantum dots and modifies the photoluminescence (PL) emission. The second reason is surface functionalization on S-GQD, which results in distinct particle size than other GQDs and is responsible for a significant energy transition, leading to a blue-shift [45].

The Raman spectra of GQDs (Fig. 2G-I) exhibit two prominent bands: the D-band representing disorderliness and structural defect and the G-band resulting from the stretching motion of sp2 carbons. For GQD, the D-band and G-band appear at 1355 cm−1 and 1607 cm−1, respectively. The D-band signifies vibrational stretching and crystallinity quality, while the G-band is associated with the crystalline nature and the E2 g vibration of the sp2 hybridized C atoms [46]. The intensity ratio of these bands (ID/IG) is 0.87, higher than that of the source GO (0.97), due to the reduction effect (Table S3). For N-GQD, the D and G bands were observed at 1382 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1, respectively. For S-GQD, they appear at 1342 cm−1 and 1592 cm−1. The doping and functionalization lead to an increase in the ID/IG ratio compared to GQD, with values of 1.03 (N-GQD) and 1.15 (S-GQD). A higher ID/IG ratio indicates more disorder and less graphitization, while a lower ID/IG ratio indicates higher graphitization. Since the GQD has the lowest ID/IG ratio than the doped variants, we conclude that GQD attains higher degree of graphitization, while S-GQD possess more amorphous characteristics due to higher ID/IG ratio.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The difference in fluorescence emission and lifetime
ce:section.ce:para: We also analyzed the fluorescence properties of GQDs using various excitation wavelengths ranging from 350 to 430 nm. When excited at 350 nm, GQDs exhibited a broad fluorescence peak (FL) at ca. 500 nm ( Fig. 3A). This broadness is attributed to the fluorescence band of different O-containing functional groups [42]. At an excitation of 370 nm, GQDs displayed the highest intensity FL at ca. 525 nm, with a Stokes shift of 155 nm (1.0 eV). Overall, an excitation-dependent FL pattern was observed for GQD. A change in excitation wavelength from 350 to 370 nm resulted in a red shift of 25 nm (0.12 eV). However, there was no significant shift in the FL, with further increases in the excitation wavelength (up to 430 nm). Notably, a decrease in intensity in the FL was observed when the excitation wavelength was increased from 370 to 430 nm, indicating a hypochromic shift.

When N-GQD is excited at different wavelengths, its FL pattern exhibits variation in intensity without a specific trend. A decrease in intensity is observed when the excitation changes from 350 to 370 nm, followed by a hyperchromic shift (up to 410 nm) and a sudden reduction in the intensity at 430 nm. The maximum FL intensity for N-GQD occurs at 410 nm, with the FL position at 484 nm. The resulting Stokes shift value is 74 nm (0.46 eV). Overall, the successive FL with increasing excitation demonstrates a redshift of 40 nm (Fig. 3B). In contrast, S-GQD displays an entirely different FL pattern when excited with a similar range of wavelengths. A hypochromic shift is observed when the excitation changes from 350 to 430 nm. The emission peak of S-GQD is situated at 430 nm (excitation at 350 nm), with a Stokes shift of 80 nm (0.66 eV). The change in the excitation wavelength from 350 to 390 nm does not yield any significant shift (around 5 nm) in FL. Further excitation at 410 and 430 nm results in a notable reduction in intensity and a strong red shift of 45 nm (0.27 eV) (Fig. 3C).

We conducted time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy using a 375 nm excitation wavelength, resulting in an emission of around 525 nm. The average lifetime for GQD is 0.64 ns (Fig. 3D), which is shorter than that of doped GQDs (Fig. 3E and F) due to differences in their electronic and photophysical properties. For N-GQD, the average lifetime is 2.10 ns, increased by promoting n-π* transition, which facilitates internal conversion and inter-system crossing. Meanwhile, in S-GQD, the average lifetime is 0.94 ns. In this case, doping in GQDs increases the fluorescence lifetime by promoting n-π* transition and easing internal conversion and inter-system crossing (ISC) [47]. The longer lifetime for N-GQD reflects more defect states, which slow down excitons recombination. The faster decay of excitons in S-GQD is due to the introduction of quenching sites from S-doping.

The quantum yield (QY) measurement, using an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, demonstrates a higher value for doped GQDs (Table S4). This increased QY facilitates radiative transitions and leads to a population of effective excitons in the triplet state [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical spectroscopy-based interaction of drugs with GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: We conducted investigated into the biophysical effects of two anticancerous drugs, MTX and FA, in conjugation with GQDs for potential drug delivery applications. The interactions were analyzed using a UV–vis and FL measurement, as illustrated in Fig. 4. The UV–vis spectra for MTX-GQD revealed two peaks at 350 and 370 nm, indicating a subtle hyperchromic shift due to π-π* transitions (Fig. 4A). The absorbance pattern for N-GQD showed a similar trend to the FA-N-GQD (Fig. 4G). Four characteristic peaks were noted at 210, 258, 300, and 375 nm; however, the high-energy peak remained unaffected upon the addition of MTX (Fig. 4B). In the S-GQD system, two distinct peaks were observed at 227 nm (sharp) and 305 nm (broad). The electronic transition in the MTX-S-GQD system showed a significant rise in intensity (Fig. 4C), while the FA-S-GQD system exhibited noticeable n-π* transitions. Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra upon the addition of MTX showed quenching for all three GQD variants (Fig. 4D-F), indicating a dynamic quenching process where the drugs interact with GQDs in the excited state. The process involves collision quenching and influencing the fluorescence spectra.

Mixing FA with GQDs in 20 μl increments leads to a gradual increase in absorbance. The sharp peak of GQD at 350 nm remains mostly unaffected, although there is a slight shift in wavelength by 5–10 nm after adding 160 μl of FA (Fig. 4G). When N-GQD shows an absorbance peak at 351 nm, adding FA produces four peaks at 210, 255, 282, and 370 nm, with a noticeable hyperchromic shift, except for the high energy peak at 210 nm (Fig. 4H). Regarding S-GQD, there are no changes observed upon the addition of FA in the π-π* transitions (Fig. 4I). However, in the region from 300 to 400 nm, corresponding to the n-π* transitions, a hyperchromic trend is noticeable, suggesting a weak interaction. Absorbance shift indicating intercalation is absent, implying that FA interacts on the surface [49]. We also analyzed the emission spectra with the incremental addition of FA. Both GQD and S-GQD show quenching (reduced intensity) (Fig. 4J and L), while N-GQD shows an opposite trend (Fig. 4K). The quenching effect is attributed to dynamic behavior resulting from collisions between FA and GQD or S-GQD, as described by Zu et al. [50]. Additionally, for S-GQD, other factors, such as electronic structure and surface property alterations due to the dopant, come into play. In contrast, the increased fluorescence with N-GQD implies the formation of a stable complex, culminating in a non-radiative decay pathway.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Collision dynamics between FA/MTX with N-GQD differs from GQD and S-GQD
ce:section.ce:para: We have confirmed through optical measurement that neither FA nor MTX intercalate between graphene sheets. To compare the dynamic properties of GQD and doped variants in the presence of drugs, we employed classical mechanics. Our analysis revealed that both GQD and S-GQD, in the presence of FA and MTX, display enhanced dynamicity (blue bars, Fig. 5A-C). In contrast, N-GQD exhibits higher average root mean squared deviation (RMSD) is higher in the absence of drugs, while the complexes show reduced bins. The distinct behavior aligns with the discrepancies observed in optical measurements. Essentially, the changes in surface properties and the collision dynamics between FA/MTX and N-GQDs (red bars, Fig. 5B and C), explain the UV–vis pattern observed (Fig. 4B and H).

We further conducted an analysis of the molecular density profiles perpendicular to the interface, providing valuable insights into the distribution of GQDs in the presence of drugs (Fig. 5D-F) [31,51]. At the GQD interfaces, a bulk region of high density near 0 nm indicated dispersion of GQDs with minimum or no interaction with the surrounding medium [52]. The maximum density value was around 300 kg m−3 for N-GQD, slightly lower for GQD and S-GQD. Upon the introduction of FA and MTX (Fig. 5D), a slight decrease in density was observed, reflecting alterations in the molecular distribution and indicating collision dynamics with the drugs. Notably, the N-GQD system exhibited a significant shift in relative density towards positive values with FA and negative with MTX. This overall density profile suggests an effective reduction in the spatial distribution per unit volume when FA and MTX are loaded onto GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretical calculations show that the binding of MTX is stronger than that of FA
ce:section.ce:para: We examined the disparity in the binding behavior of both drugs by correlating the number of H-bonds and the contact area with the GQDs. We observed that MTX forms more H-bonds than FA (Fig. 5G), although GQD forms more H-bonds in the presence of FA. However, the total contact area is smaller for FA compared to MTX. Conversely, S-GQD covers the largest contact area compared to N-GQD, indicating a pronounced interaction with MTX (Fig. 5H). These differences in the binding pattern arise from the surface properties and may be crucial for the potential role of GQDs in targeted drug delivery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQD shows significant cytotoxic activity compared to N-GQD and S-GQD
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the cytotoxicity effects of GQDs, MTT assay was conducted on HeLa cervical cancer cells and HEK normal cells. The results of the MTX treatment after 48 hours are available in Fig. S3. The IC50 values for GQD, N-GQD, and S-GQD treatment of HeLa cells were determined to be 0.08, 0.98, and 109 μg/mL, respectively ( Fig. 6A-C and Table 1). The increased toxicity of GQD is likely due to its heightened graphitization, resulting in strong hydrophobic interactions with the cellular membrane and enhanced cellular uptake compared to N-GQD and S-GQD. Furthermore, the introduction of N- and S-doping reduces the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups, leading to a reduced generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS). This, in turn, is linked to cellular damage and toxicity. Additionally, doping also helps to diminish molecular aggregation and lessens cytotoxicity, contributing to reduced toxic effects from the doped GQDs [53]. The dopants in GQDs also contributes to structural defects that impact interactions with cellular membranes, causing more disruption to membrane integrity [54] by removing the lipid bilayer compared to N-GQD and S-GQD [55,56]. When HEK cells were subjected to varying concentrations, no significant cytotoxicity was observed with S-GQD (Fig. 6D-F). However, high doses of GQD and N-GQD displayed mild toxicity. Nonetheless, these findings underscore the potential anticancer activity of GQD and its doped variants.

The clinical use of MTX is limited as cancer cells develop resistance [57]. We hypothesized to explore whether combining GQD with MTX could enhance cancer therapy effectiveness despite GQD’s higher toxicity. We conducted an MTT assay to investigate the impact of combined GQD-MTX treatment on cancer cell viability.

Our results showed that using GQD IC50–MTX IC50 resulted in only 5.6 % of viable cells, while GQD IC25–MTX IC75 and GQD IC75–MTX IC25 resulted in 41 % and 26.5 % viability, respectively (Fig. 6G). The high density of functional groups on GQD’s surface played a crucial role in the cytotoxicity. Previous reports suggested that GQDs functionalized with COOH, amino, or sulphonic groups show minimum cytotoxicity and are biocompatible, but hydroxylated GQDs show significant toxicity against cancer cells [58–60]. However, in our results, we also observed cytotoxicity in normal cells, indicating the need for further dose optimization. Cells treated with N-GQD IC50–MTX IC50, N-GQD IC25–MTX IC75, and N-GQD IC75–MTX IC25 showed viabilities of 45 %, 47 % and 58.6 %, respectively (Fig. 6H). Similarly, cells treated with S-GQD IC50–MTX IC50, S-GQD IC25–MTX IC75, and S-GQD IC75–MTX IC25 showed viabilities of 41 %, 57 %, and 36 %, respectively (Fig. 6I). While mild toxicity was observed after 48 hrs of treatment with N-GQD and S-GQD in combination with MTX, it was clear that they had only a limited role in enhancing MTX activity. However, no significant cytotoxic impact was observed in normal cells with these dopants, suggesting their biocompatible nature (Fig. 6J-L).

Furthermore, we examined the combined impact of FA and GQDs-MTX on tumor cell viability. When For the FA-GQD IC50–MTX IC50, only 35 % of cells remained viable. Similarly, the treatments involving FA-GQD IC25–MTX IC75 and FA-GQD IC75–MTX IC25 resulted in 39 % and 36.5 % cell viability, respectively (Fig. S3A). For the FA-N-GQD IC50–MTX IC50, FA-N-GQD IC25–MTX IC75, and FA-N-GQD IC75–MTX IC25 treatments, cells viability was observed at 50 %, 61 % and 69 %, respectively (Fig. S3B). In the case of FA-S-GQD IC50–MTX IC50, FA-S-GQD IC25–MTX IC75, and FA-S-GQD IC75–MTX IC25 treatments, the cell viability was 49 %, 61 %, and 49 %, respectively (Fig. S3C). These results strongly suggest that the FA conjugate GQDs-MTX does not significantly alter the impact. This may be attributed to the presence of FA, which could potentially enable a prolonged and sustained release of MTX at the targeted site by masking its release from the quantum dots.

We synthesized pristine GQD and two doped variants, N-GQD and S-GQD, hydrothermally from GO. Our systematic investigation of the optical, structural, and morphological properties of these zero-dimensional materials revealed their potential application in cancer therapy. We utilized optical characterization methods, including UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy, to identify distinct electronic transitions and band gaps among the variants. Our comparative analysis showed that S-GQD demonstrates a noticeably higher band gap and blue shift due to its smaller particle size. Furthermore, time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy indicated longer lifetimes for doped GQDs, attributed to enhanced n-π* transitions and ISC.

A combined study of UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy examined the interaction between GQDs and the anticancerous drug MTX. These results revealed non-intercalative surface interactions, which were corroborated by molecular dynamics simulations. Through trajectory analysis, distinct collision dynamics and density profiles were observed in the presence of drugs. In cell viability assays, pristine GQD exhibited notable efficiency in killing cancer cells, attributed to their heightened graphitization and hydrophobic interaction, facilitating enhanced cellular uptake. Additionally, combined treatment with GQD and MTX significantly reduced cancer cell viability, highlighting the potential to enhance therapeutic efficacy. N-GQD and S-GQD displayed limited MTX activity but demonstrated biocompatible characteristics.

The current study has some limitations, including challenges in achieving a uniform-size distribution of GQDs, which can be improved by optimizing the synthesis condition to tune its optoelectronic properties. Additionally, the lack of scalability in synthesis process is a hurdle for commercialization of GQD-based cancer therapeutics. Furthermore, the need of functionalization to tailor the optical characteristics of GQDs presents scope of improvement, which requires additional synthesis steps and control of structural defects. In the future, doped GQDs can be studied for drug loading and release characteristics to improve targeted drug delivery. Their remarkable photostability and adjustable fluorescence offers unique opportunities for bioimaging applications. Moreover, their ability to interact with biological systems and their biocompatibility characteristics make them as ideal candidates for regenerative medicine and tissue engineering applications.

Overall, this work brings new findings in following ways: (i) It explores GQDs for improved biocompatibility and reduced cytotoxicity, opening opportunities for advancing targeted cancer therapy. (ii) The study reveals synergistic interaction of GQDs with anticancer drugs, demonstrating non-intercalative interactions, and has potential implication in cancer treatment outcomes. (iii) Our interdisciplinary approach combines experimental characterization, molecular computation, and biological assays, providing a deeper understanding of GQD behaviour. Certainly, it paves way for future nanomaterial-based cancer theranostics.
